papers.title: A hybrid machine learning framework with two-step feature selection for identifying key biomarkers and drug targets in monkeypox.
papers.abstract: Monkeypox (Mpox) is a viral disease that has garnered global attention due to its human-to-human transmissibility and cross-species transmission. Recent outbreaks in both endemic and non-endemic regions highlight the urgent need to elucidate its molecular mechanisms and evolutionary dynamics. As of now, there are no approved antiviral therapies available for the treatment of Mpox. In this work, we proposed a robust machine learning (ML) and bioinformatics approach to uncover candidate biomarkers associated with Mpox. Two microarray datasets and one RNA-seq dataset were analyzed to screen an in
papers.summary: This study uses a hybrid machine learning framework with two-step feature selection to identify key biomarkers and potential drug targets for monkeypox (Mpox). By analyzing multiple gene expression datasets, the approach aims to uncover molecular mechanisms and candidate biomarkers for this viral disease, for which no approved antiviral therapies currently exist.

papers.title: A senescence-based machine learning model prognosticates and personalizes therapy in cervical cancer.
papers.abstract: Cervical cancer remains a major global health burden. This study aimed to characterize the role of cellular senescence in the tumor microenvironment (TME) and its clinical implications, filling the critical gap in understanding senescence landscapes in cervical cancer.
papers.summary: This study developed a machine learning model based on cellular senescence genes to predict prognosis and guide personalized therapy for cervical cancer patients. By analyzing the senescence landscape in the tumor microenvironment, the model helps identify patients who may benefit from specific treatments.

papers.title: Artificial intelligence-based models for colorectal cancer diagnosis using laboratory tests: an exploratory retrospective case-control study.
papers.abstract: Colorectal cancer (CRC) is frequently diagnosed at advanced stages owing to the insidious nature of its early clinical manifestations and the lack of validated screening modalities. This diagnostic delay presents a substantial therapeutic challenge, underscoring the need to develop population-based screening strategies. Based on laboratory data, this study constructed a diagnostic model for CRC to explore a screening strategy for population-based tumor census.
papers.summary: This study developed an AI-based diagnostic model for colorectal cancer using routine laboratory test data, aiming to improve early detection and population screening. The model shows potential as a non-invasive, cost-effective screening tool, though it requires further validation in prospective settings.

papers.title: Multi-omics identification of an antibody dependent cellular phagocytosis related signature for hepatocellular carcinoma.
papers.abstract: The reduced phagocytic activity of macrophages in the tumor microenvironment (TME) and the presence of anti-phagocytic factors in cancer cells hinder the effectiveness of immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) in clinical settings. Consequently, our objective was to create a risk assessment model using antibody-dependent cellular phagocytosis (ADCP) regulators to serve as prognostic indicators and immunotherapy markers for individuals with hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC).
papers.summary: This study developed a risk assessment model based on antibody-dependent cellular phagocytosis (ADCP) regulators to predict prognosis and immunotherapy response in hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC). The model aims to address the challenge of reduced macrophage phagocytic activity in the tumor microenvironment, which limits the effectiveness of immune checkpoint inhibitors.

papers.title: Accuracy of machine learning in detecting lymph node metastasis of esophageal cancer: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
papers.abstract: Currently, the detection of early lymph node metastasis in esophageal cancer during preoperative assessments is still challenging. Machine learning has been used in the detection of early lymph node metastasis in esophageal cancer. However, its detection accuracy remains controversial. Therefore, this systematic review and meta-analysis aimed to explore the accuracy of machine learning in the detection of lymph node metastasis in esophageal cancer.
papers.summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis evaluates the accuracy of machine learning models for detecting lymph node metastasis in esophageal cancer. The study synthesizes existing evidence to clarify the diagnostic performance of these models, which remains a topic of debate.

papers.title: Exploration and experimental validation of oxidative stress-related diagnostic genes in interstitial cystitis based on transcriptomics.
papers.abstract: Interstitial cystitis (IC) is a chronic pain syndrome with an elusive diagnosis and poorly understood pathogenesis, in which oxidative stress (OS) is increasingly implicated. This study aimed to identify and validate OS-related diagnostic biomarkers for IC using an integrative computational and experimental approach.
papers.summary: This study identifies and validates oxidative stress-related genes as potential diagnostic biomarkers for interstitial cystitis by combining computational analysis of transcriptomic data with experimental confirmation. The findings suggest that these genes could improve diagnosis and understanding of this chronic pain syndrome.

papers.title: Mining and experimental validation of machine learning-based immune-related diagnostic biomarkers for hepatocellular carcinoma.
papers.abstract: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is a highly malignant and aggressive tumor. Immune-related genes (IRGs) expression correlates closely with the HCC immune microenvironment, and this study aims to identify immune-related diagnostic markers in HCC.
papers.summary: This study uses machine learning to identify immune-related genes that could serve as diagnostic biomarkers for hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC), a common liver cancer. The findings were validated experimentally, suggesting potential new targets for early diagnosis and treatment of HCC.

papers.title: Development and external validation of machine learning models to predict insulin resistance among iron-deficient children and adolescents.
papers.abstract: Insulin resistance (IR) represents a critical metabolic complication in iron-deficient children, yet existing predictive models target general or overweight pediatric populations and do not account for iron-deficiency as a distinct risk modifier. This study aimed to develop and externally validate machine learning (ML) models using routinely available clinical parameters to address this diagnostic gap.
papers.summary: This study developed and validated machine learning models to predict insulin resistance specifically in iron-deficient children and adolescents, addressing a gap where existing models target general or overweight populations. Using routine clinical parameters, the models showed promise for identifying metabolic risk in this understudied group.

papers.title: Deep learning model based on MRI-derived microvascular network simulation parameters for noninvasive assessment of lymphovascular invasion in rectal cancer patients.
papers.abstract: Accurate preoperative assessment of lymphovascular invasion (LVI) in patients with rectal cancer (RC) is important for guiding postoperative management. This study aimed to develop and validate a deep learning model based on magnetic resonance imaging (MRI)-derived microvascular network simulation parameters for the preoperative assessment of LVI in RC patients.
papers.summary: This study developed a deep learning model using MRI-derived microvascular network simulation parameters to noninvasively assess lymphovascular invasion (LVI) in rectal cancer patients before surgery. The model aims to improve preoperative evaluation, which is critical for guiding postoperative treatment decisions. Results suggest that combining microvascular imaging features with deep learning can enhance LVI prediction accuracy.

papers.title: AFD-Net: a robust exchange rate forecasting framework integrating frequency-domain decomposition and attention mechanisms with linear modeling.
papers.abstract: Exchange rate forecasting remains a fundamental challenge in financial time series analysis due to the inherent non-linearity, non-stationarity, and complex temporal dynamics of currency markets. This paper introduces AFD-Net (Attention-Enhanced FAN with DLinear), a novel deep learning framework designed to synergistically integrate frequency-domain decomposition, attention mechanisms, and linear time series modeling. The key architectural innovation lies in the sequential pipeline design: the FAN module first transforms non-stationary inputs into stationary residuals, enabling the subsequent
papers.summary: This paper presents AFD-Net, a deep learning framework that combines frequency-domain decomposition, attention mechanisms, and linear modeling to improve exchange rate forecasting. The method first transforms non-stationary financial data into stationary residuals, then applies attention and linear models to capture complex temporal patterns. The framework shows robust performance on exchange rate prediction tasks.

papers.title: VITAGRAPH: building a knowledge graph for biologically relevant learning tasks.
papers.abstract: The complexity of human biology poses ongoing challenges, driving global interdisciplinary research. Artificial intelligence has become a powerful tool in computational biology, where graph data structures model entities like protein-protein interaction (PPI) networks and gene functional networks. These networks support crucial tasks in network medicine, including gene-disease association prediction, drug repurposing, and polypharmacy side-effect analysis. Reliable machine learning predictions require high-quality data. We present VITAGRAPH, a comprehensive multi-purpose biological knowledge g
papers.summary: VITAGRAPH is a new comprehensive biological knowledge graph that integrates diverse data types to support machine learning tasks in network medicine, such as predicting gene-disease associations and drug repurposing. By providing high-quality, structured data, it aims to improve the reliability of AI-driven predictions in computational biology.

papers.title: Integrative multi-omics reveals MHC class II-mediated neuroinflammation and systemic metabolic dysregulation as transdiagnostic drivers in major brain disorders.
papers.abstract: Psychiatric, neurodevelopmental, and neurodegenerative disorders, including Alzheimer's disease (AD), attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), autism spectrum disorder (ASD), bipolar disorder (BIP), major depressive disorder (MDD), and schizophrenia (SCZ), exhibit complex etiologies driven by immune and metabolic dysregulation. While distinct in their clinical onset, these conditions share overlapping molecular vulnerabilities. This study pioneers an integrative multi-omics framework, combining multi-tissue TWAS, cross-disorder pleiotropy analyses, Mendelian Randomization (MR), predict
papers.summary: This study uses an integrative multi-omics approach across multiple brain disorders (Alzheimer's, ADHD, autism, bipolar disorder, depression, and schizophrenia) to identify shared molecular drivers. The authors find that MHC class II-mediated neuroinflammation and systemic metabolic dysregulation are common transdiagnostic mechanisms underlying these conditions.

papers.title: Prediction of axillary lymph node metastasis using a transformer model and multi-omics validation in breast cancer.
papers.abstract: Our study developed a multiomics-driven transformer model that combines mammography, MRI, transcriptomic and proteomic data to noninvasively predict axillary lymph node (ALN) metastasis in breast cancer. A total of 2105 patients from 10 institutions were included for model training and validation. The model achieved an AUC of 0.939 in the training cohort (n = 658) and 0.830-0.867 across three independent validation cohorts (n = 282, 971 and 194, respectively), outperforming conventional ultrasound examination. Grad-CAM visualizations highlighted the tumor edges and surrounding tissue, consiste
papers.summary: This study developed a transformer model integrating mammography, MRI, transcriptomic, and proteomic data to noninvasively predict axillary lymph node metastasis in breast cancer, achieving high AUC across multiple validation cohorts. The model outperformed conventional ultrasound and highlighted tumor edges and surrounding tissue as key predictive features.

papers.title: Decoding promoter activity from DNA sequence using pre-trained language models.
papers.abstract: Promoter architecture plays a central role in transcriptional regulation, but predicting promoter activity directly from DNA sequence remains challenging. Here, we tested whether transformer-based DNA language models can learn regulatory logic encoded in Drosophila core promoters. We fine-tuned the pretrained DNA language model DNABERT-2 using a synthetic core promoter dataset measured in S2 cells with luciferase reporter assays. The model predicted promoter activity well when biological replicates were split between training and test data (R² ≈ 0.91), and retained meaningful performance when
papers.summary: This study shows that a transformer-based DNA language model (DNABERT-2) can predict promoter activity from DNA sequence in Drosophila cells, achieving high accuracy when training and test data come from the same biological replicates. The model's performance drops when tested on held-out replicates, highlighting the challenge of generalizing across biological variability. This work demonstrates the potential of pre-trained language models for decoding regulatory logic from sequence alone.

papers.title: Impact of QTL number, heritability and reference population size on the benefit of machine learning models over GBLUP in genomic prediction.
papers.abstract: With the successful application of machine learning (ML) in many areas, its potential for genomic prediction (GP) in animal and plant breeding has attracted growing attention. However, despite numerous studies, the benefit of ML models over GBLUP remains controversial. Some studies reported higher accuracy of ML than linear models with small reference populations, but this benefit often disappeared in larger datasets. Additionally, the number of QTL also affects the prediction accuracy of ML models. The aim of this study was to compare the performance of three ML models, random forest (RF), su
papers.summary: This study compares machine learning models (random forest, support vector regression, and gradient boosting) against the standard GBLUP method for genomic prediction in breeding, finding that ML models only outperform GBLUP when the number of quantitative trait loci (QTL) is small and the reference population size is limited. As the number of QTL increases or the reference population grows, GBLUP matches or exceeds ML performance, suggesting that ML benefits are context-dependent rather than universal.

papers.title: A vision-based MobileNet-GRU framework for continuous monitoring of student engagement and emotional states.
papers.abstract: Student engagement and emotional state monitoring remains a major challenge in modern education, particularly in online learning environments where direct instructor supervision is limited. Conventional evaluation approaches, such as surveys, quizzes, and feedback forms, rely heavily on subjective judgment and often fail to capture the dynamic temporal patterns of learner attention and behavioral engagement patterns. To address this limitation, this study proposes a vision-based framework that integrates efficient spatial feature extraction with temporal sequence modeling for continuous engage
papers.summary: This paper presents a vision-based framework combining MobileNet and GRU to continuously monitor student engagement and emotional states in online learning, addressing the limitations of subjective evaluation methods. The approach captures dynamic temporal patterns of learner attention and behavior, offering a potential tool for real-time feedback in educational settings.

papers.title: CPP2Vec: A representation learning approach for cell-penetrating peptides prediction.
papers.abstract: Cell-penetrating peptides (CPPs) facilitate the delivery of a variety of therapeutic molecules across the plasma membrane, from small chemical substances to nucleic acid-based macromolecules, such as antisense oligonucleotides (ASOs). Among neutral ASOs, peptide nucleic acids (PNAs) and phosphorodiamidate morpholino oligomers (PMOs) have been extensively studied as potential medical treatments for Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy (DMD), a severe genetic disease that causes muscle degeneration progressively. Over the last few decades, many in silico methods have emerged to detect novel CPPs, counter
papers.summary: The paper introduces CPP2Vec, a new machine learning method that uses representation learning to predict cell-penetrating peptides (CPPs), which are important for delivering therapeutic molecules like antisense oligonucleotides into cells. This approach aims to improve the accuracy of computational CPP prediction compared to existing in silico methods.

papers.title: Ensemble machine learning for predicting breast cancer recurrence and mortality using clinical and hemogram data.
papers.abstract: Accurate prediction of breast cancer recurrence remains difficult because prognosis varies significantly across molecular subtypes. This underscores the need for scalable, affordable, and interpretable prognostic tools. We developed machine learning models integrating hematological indices with clinicopathologic data to predict 2- and 10-year recurrence or all-case death. We retrospectively analyzed 4277 women with primary breast cancer (2008-2022). The cohort included hormone receptor-positive (HR + ), HER2-positive, and triple-negative (TNBC) subtypes. We trained multiple classifiers and int
papers.summary: This study developed machine learning models that combine blood test results with clinical data to predict breast cancer recurrence and death within 2 and 10 years. Using data from over 4,000 patients across different breast cancer subtypes, the ensemble approach showed improved accuracy and interpretability for prognosis. The findings suggest that adding routine hematological indices can enhance affordable, scalable prognostic tools for breast cancer.

papers.title: Uncovering the mechanism of DCLK3 in colorectal cancer progression through WGCNA and machine learning.
papers.abstract: Colorectal cancer (CRC) is a prevalent malignant tumor of the digestive tract with high morbidity and mortality rates. Although doublecortin like kinase 3 (DCLK3) is upregulated in CRC and promotes disease progression, its specific mechanisms remain unclear.
papers.summary: This study uses WGCNA and machine learning to investigate the role of DCLK3 in colorectal cancer progression, finding that DCLK3 is upregulated and promotes tumor growth. The research provides insights into the molecular mechanisms by which DCLK3 drives CRC progression, though the exact pathways remain to be fully elucidated.

papers.title: Enhancing tumor T cell antigen prediction by integrating deep protein representations.
papers.abstract: Cancer is a major threat to human health, and cancer immunotherapy is considered the most promising treatment option. It offers high effectiveness and precision, with fewer side effects than traditional treatments. Tumor T cell antigens (TTCA) are the proteins or protein fragments that stand on the surface of cancer cells and that are recognized by the immune system. However, physical experimental approaches to infer them might be costly and time-consuming, even though they are important for cancer immunotherapy.
papers.summary: This paper presents a computational method that improves the prediction of tumor T cell antigens by integrating deep protein representations. The approach aims to identify which proteins on cancer cells can be recognized by the immune system, which is crucial for developing effective immunotherapies. By using deep learning on protein sequences, the method offers a faster and cheaper alternative to physical experiments.

papers.title: Serum Proteomic Profiling Implicates a Dysregulated Neurohormonal-Inflammatory Axis in Post-Fontan Sinus Tachycardia.
papers.abstract: Postoperative sinus tachycardia is a poorly understood complication following the Fontan procedure. The molecular signaling cascades triggering acute tachycardia remain uncharacterized, limiting therapeutic innovation. Here, we present a retrospective study leveraging serum proteomics and machine learning to identify the molecular drivers of postoperative Fontan sinus tachycardia.
papers.journal: Journal of the American Heart Association
papers.summary: This study uses serum proteomics and machine learning to identify molecular drivers of sinus tachycardia after the Fontan procedure, revealing a dysregulated neurohormonal-inflammatory axis. The findings provide new insights into a poorly understood complication and may guide future therapeutic strategies.

papers.title: InstaNovo-P: a de novo peptide sequencing model for phosphoproteomics.
papers.abstract: Phosphorylation, a crucial post-translational modification (PTM), plays a central role in cellular signaling and disease mechanisms. Mass spectrometry-based phosphoproteomics is widely used for system-wide characterization of phosphorylation events. However, traditional methods struggle with accurate phosphorylated site localization, complex search spaces, and detecting sequences outside the reference database. Advances in de novo peptide sequencing offer opportunities to address these limitations, but have yet to become integrated and adapted for phosphoproteomics datasets. Here, we present I
papers.summary: InstaNovo-P is a new AI model designed to directly sequence phosphorylated peptides from mass spectrometry data, overcoming limitations of traditional database-dependent methods. It improves the accuracy of identifying phosphorylation sites and can detect novel peptides not in existing reference databases, which is crucial for studying cellular signaling and disease mechanisms.

papers.title: A unified vision-language model for precision oncology and biomarker prediction in neuroblastoma.
papers.abstract: Neuroblastoma is a leading cause of childhood cancer mortality, presenting persistent management challenges due to its biological heterogeneity and the limited accessibility of molecular profiling in routine practice. Here we present NEVA (NEuroblastoma Vision-language AI), a multimodal foundation model designed to address these barriers. Unlike conventional approaches that rely on frozen encoders and multiple instance learning, NEVA implements a pathologist-inspired hierarchical workflow with end-to-end optimization. Developed and evaluated in a large multi-institutional cohort of 1,238 patie
papers.summary: Researchers developed NEVA, a multimodal AI model that analyzes both pathology images and clinical text to predict biomarkers and outcomes in neuroblastoma, a childhood cancer. Trained on over 1,200 patients, NEVA outperforms conventional methods by using an end-to-end hierarchical approach inspired by pathologists. This work could make precision oncology more accessible by reducing reliance on costly molecular profiling.

papers.title: Decoding cancer circulating transcriptomic signatures with language models.
papers.abstract: Current liquid biopsy methods for multi-cancer detection using plasma cell-free RNA (cfRNA, short RNA fragments circulating in blood that can reflect disease states) typically rely on gene annotations, which can overlook signals from unannotated or repetitive genomic regions. We present GeneLLM, a Transformer-based model that directly processes the nucleotide sequences of human-mapped cfRNA reads to identify cancer-indicative signatures. By bypassing gene-level quantification, the model retains signals from transcriptomic dark matter. The model learns latent pseudo-biomarkers (prototype repres
papers.summary: GeneLLM is a new AI model that directly analyzes the DNA sequences of RNA fragments in blood to detect cancer, bypassing the need for traditional gene annotations. This approach captures signals from previously ignored genomic 'dark matter', potentially improving multi-cancer detection from a simple blood test.

papers.title: Mind the gap: An embedding guide to safely travel in sequence space.
papers.abstract: We present a hybrid approach combining a protein language model (pLM) with Monte Carlo (MC) sampling for generating enzyme mutants free of mutations deleterious for structural preservation. Given the amino acid sequence of the original enzyme and a set of residues for which the local environment should be conserved, i.e., the catalytic site, our approach generates mutants that differ vastly in the overall sequence while retaining the geometry of the conserved region, thereby representing promising candidates for further experimental screening. Unlike end-to-end deep-learning approaches, whose
papers.summary: This paper introduces a hybrid method that combines a protein language model with Monte Carlo sampling to generate enzyme mutants that preserve the geometry of catalytic sites while allowing large changes elsewhere in the sequence. The approach produces diverse mutants that are structurally sound and promising for experimental screening, offering an alternative to end-to-end deep learning methods.

papers.title: LoMuS: Low-Rank Adaptation with Sequence Multi-representation Improves Protein Stability Prediction.
papers.abstract: Protein folding stability is a key determinant for understanding protein dynamics, including molecular function, pathogenicity, and protein engineering. Yet, accurate prediction of protein stability remains challenging due to high variability in available data, particularly when only sequence information is available and structural knowledge is limited or unavailable. In this work, we introduce LoMuS, a multi-representation-based deep learning model that predicts dataset-provided protein stability scores directly from the primary sequence. In the core of the model architecture, a fusion networ
papers.summary: LoMuS is a new deep learning model that predicts protein stability directly from amino acid sequences, even when structural information is unavailable. It uses a multi-representation approach with a fusion network to handle the high variability in stability data, improving prediction accuracy.

papers.title: RLAnOxPeptide: An Integrated Framework Combining Transformer and reinforcement learning for Efficient Antioxidant Peptide Prediction and Innovative Design.
papers.abstract: Bioactive peptides exhibit immense potential in pharmaceutical and food science domains, with antioxidant peptides (AOPs) garnering significant attention for their roles in scavenging free radicals. However, traditional discovery methods are inefficient and costly. This study introduces RLAnOxPeptide, an integrated computational framework that merges machine learning and reinforcement learning for the efficient prediction and de novo design of AOPs.
papers.summary: This paper presents RLAnOxPeptide, a computational framework that combines a transformer model with reinforcement learning to predict and design new antioxidant peptides. The approach aims to overcome the high cost and inefficiency of traditional experimental discovery methods for these bioactive peptides.

papers.title: Machine learning reveals mechanisms and feedstock effects on potassium and magnesium uptake by wheat in biochar-amended soil in greenhouse.
papers.abstract: Despite the potential of biochar to enhance crop nutrient uptake, the complex, feedstock-dependent interactions among biochar properties, soil biological processes, and plant root traits remain poorly understood. Hence, this study employed machine learning algorithms (Linear regression and Random Forest) with advanced feature selection methods (Correlation-based Feature Subset Selection Evaluator, Correlation-Based Attribute Evaluator, Principal Components Attribute Transformer, and Stepwise Regression) to predict potassium (K+) and magnesium (Mg2+) uptake by wheat (Triticum aestivum L.) roots
papers.summary: This study uses machine learning to predict how different biochar feedstocks affect potassium and magnesium uptake in wheat roots. The models reveal complex interactions between biochar properties, soil biology, and plant roots that were previously poorly understood.

papers.title: GenART: Reading the genome's language with adaptive "words".
papers.abstract: Tokenization is both a prerequisite and a central challenge for genomic language models, due to the inherent difficulty of delineating meaningful segments within continuous DNA sequences. We present GenART, a genomic language model framework that dynamically segments DNA into variable-length words directly from raw sequences without manual annotation. Its key adaptive tokenization module infers biologically meaningful boundaries from multi-scale contextual signals during pretraining. GenART demonstrates competitive performance across diverse tasks, outperforming leading approaches. Word bounda
papers.summary: GenART is a new genomic language model that learns to automatically segment DNA sequences into variable-length 'words' without needing manual annotation, unlike previous methods that use fixed tokenization. By dynamically identifying biologically meaningful boundaries from context during training, it outperforms existing approaches on a variety of genomic tasks. This adaptive tokenization could improve how AI models understand the functional grammar of DNA.

papers.title: Prediction of in-hospital atrial fibrillation after acute myocardial infarction: a cross-cohort study in Italian and Finnish patients.
papers.abstract: The development of atrial fibrillation (AF) during hospitalization for acute myocardial infarction (AMI) is common and associated with worse prognosis. We aimed to train and, for the first time, cross-nationally validate a machine learning model to predict AF developing during the cardiac intensive care unit (CICU) stay in post-AMI patients admitted in sinus rhythm, using only routine, non-electrocardiographic clinical parameters available at CICU admission.
papers.summary: This study developed a machine learning model to predict atrial fibrillation in patients hospitalized for acute myocardial infarction, using only routine clinical data available at admission. The model was validated across Italian and Finnish patient cohorts, demonstrating its potential for early risk stratification in cardiac intensive care units.

papers.title: AbTune: layer-wise selective fine-tuning of protein language models for antibodies.
papers.abstract: Antibodies play central roles in immune defense and are widely used as therapeutic agents. However, the high structural and sequence diversity of antigen-binding loops, combined with limited experimental data and weak co-evolutionary signals, makes it difficult to develop generalizable predictive models. In this work, we investigate test-time fine-tuning strategies to improve protein language model (pLM) performance in low-data settings, with a focus on antibody-related tasks. Systematic evaluations across tasks show that carefully constrained fine-tuning greatly enhances performance while pre
papers.summary: This paper introduces AbTune, a method for selectively fine-tuning protein language models on antibody tasks by constraining which layers are updated during training. The approach improves model performance in low-data settings by focusing on the most relevant parts of the model, addressing challenges posed by antibody diversity and limited experimental data.

papers.title: Advancing bioinformatics with language models: components, applications, and perspectives.
papers.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are deep learning-based artificial intelligence models that have achieved remarkable success in natural language processing. Typically composed of neural networks with billions of parameters, they are trained on massive unlabeled datasets using self-supervised or semi-supervised learning. Beyond language, LLMs hold immense potential for addressing complex bioinformatics challenges. This review provides a comprehensive overview of transformer-based model applications in genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, drug discovery, and single-cell analysis. We discuss criti
papers.summary: This review provides a comprehensive overview of how large language models (LLMs), originally developed for natural language processing, are being applied to bioinformatics challenges including genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, drug discovery, and single-cell analysis. The authors discuss the components and training of transformer-based models and their potential to address complex biological problems.

papers.title: RD-OMICS: An Integrative Multi-Omics Data Inventory in Rare Diseases.
papers.abstract: Rare diseases (RD) impact over 30 million individuals in the United States, yet fewer than 5% of the identified conditions have FDA-approved treatments. Progress in RD research is hindered by small patient cohorts, biological heterogeneity, and the fragmented, inconsistently annotated publicly available omics data, which limits integrative analysis and translational discovery. Here, we present RD-OMICS, a data inventory with integrated and structured RD omics data from Gene Expression Omnibus (GEO), in the form of a knowledge graph. We developed a metadata harmonization pipeline that combines
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: RD-OMICS is a new knowledge graph that integrates and structures rare disease omics data from public repositories, addressing the fragmentation and inconsistent annotation that hinder research. This resource aims to facilitate integrative analysis and translational discovery for rare diseases, where fewer than 5% have FDA-approved treatments.

papers.title: Affinage: genome-scale mechanistic gene annotation from the published literature.
papers.abstract: Understanding the mechanistic function of a gene is a critical starting point for biology. However, for much of the human proteome that knowledge is scattered across thousands of primary papers or remains poorly established, while the curated databases biologists rely on can lag years behind recent literature. Large language models can now read and synthesize that literature on demand, but doing so faithfully for many genes is an expensive, non-reproducible retrieval session that does not scale across users. Here, we present Affinage, an LLM pipeline that performs this retrieval and mechanisti
papers.summary: Affinage is a new LLM-based pipeline that automatically reads and synthesizes published literature to produce mechanistic gene annotations at genome scale, addressing the gap between rapidly growing literature and slow manual curation. It aims to provide up-to-date, reproducible functional annotations for the human proteome, overcoming the scalability and cost limitations of on-demand LLM retrieval.

papers.title: Molecular Adaptations of Thermophilic Proteins-From Mechanistic Understanding to Machine Learning Approaches.
papers.abstract: Thermophilic proteins are known for their stability and activity at elevated temperatures and hence serve as useful models for engineering enzymes with enhanced stability. The stability of these proteins is governed by both thermodynamic and kinetic factors that affect the folding-unfolding equilibrium and the rate of irreversible denaturation. Structural studies have shown that enhanced thermostability often results from multiple cooperative interactions, including improved hydrophobic packing, strengthened electrostatic interactions, and optimized hydrogen-bonding networks. Comparative studi
papers.summary: This review examines how thermophilic proteins maintain stability at high temperatures through cooperative structural adaptations like improved hydrophobic packing and electrostatic interactions, and discusses how machine learning approaches are being used to understand and engineer these properties. The findings provide a framework for designing enzymes with enhanced thermal stability for industrial applications.

papers.title: A multi-chip integrated analysis employing machine learning techniques identifies biomarkers for diagnosing PCOS and its comorbidity with T2D.
papers.abstract: Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) is a common endocrine disorder closely linked to type 2 diabetes (T2D). Although linked, potential biomarkers for PCOS have not yet been sufficiently studied. This study aimed to identify diagnostic biomarkers linked to PCOS through an integrative analysis of multi-chip data and to examine their relationship with T2D. We used multi-chip datasets from public databases, conducted differential expression analysis to screen genes, and performed cross-disease gene overlap analysis using Venn diagrams. We conducted functional enrichment analyses, including gene ontol
papers.summary: This study used machine learning to analyze multiple gene expression datasets and identified potential diagnostic biomarkers for polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) and its comorbidity with type 2 diabetes (T2D). The findings may help improve diagnosis and understanding of the shared molecular mechanisms between these two conditions.

papers.title: Non-invasive diagnostic model for myocarditis using cardiac magnetic resonance radiomics.
papers.abstract: Myocarditis is an inflammatory myocardial disease with high clinical heterogeneity, and its early diagnosis relies on invasive endomyocardial biopsy (EMB) or advanced cardiac magnetic resonance (CMR) techniques with poor accessibility in primary hospitals. Conventional CMR interpretation is subjective with inter-observer variability, and single-modality radiomics models have insufficient diagnostic accuracy. This study aimed to construct and validate a machine learning model integrating multimodal CMR radiomics features and clinical parameters for early non-invasive and accurate diagnosis of m
papers.journal: Quantitative imaging in medicine and surgery
papers.summary: This study developed a machine learning model that combines multimodal cardiac magnetic resonance (CMR) radiomics features with clinical parameters to diagnose myocarditis non-invasively and accurately. The model aims to overcome the limitations of invasive biopsies and subjective CMR interpretation, potentially improving early diagnosis in settings with limited access to advanced techniques.

papers.title: Spatial-temporal and physical constrained deep learning model for simultaneous T1 and T2 reconstruction and mapping (STEP).
papers.abstract: Magnetic resonance (MR) parametric maps provide quantitative tissue characteristics that are valuable for medical diagnosis. However, existing T1 and T2 measurement techniques confront challenges such as prolonged reconstruction/fitting times and the requirement for multi-sequence image registration, limiting the clinical applicability of quantitative parameter mapping. The development of compressed sensing combined with parallel imaging technology has improved reconstruction efficiency. However, traditional two-step workflow lacks spatial constraints for parameter mapping and suffers from slo
papers.journal: Quantitative imaging in medicine and surgery
papers.summary: This paper presents a deep learning model that simultaneously reconstructs T1 and T2 parametric maps from undersampled MRI data, addressing the limitations of slow reconstruction and the need for image registration. The model incorporates spatial-temporal and physical constraints to improve accuracy and speed, potentially enhancing clinical utility of quantitative MRI.

papers.title: Learning millisecond protein dynamics from what is missing in NMR spectra.
papers.abstract: Many proteins' biological functions rely on interconversions between multiple conformations occurring at micro-to millisecond (µs-ms) timescales. A lack of standardized, large-scale experimental data has hindered obtaining a more predictive understanding of these motions. After curating >100 Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) relaxation datasets, we realized an observable for µs-ms dynamics might be hiding in plain sight. Millisecond dynamics can cause NMR signals to broaden beyond detection, leaving some residues not assigned in the chemical shift datasets of ~10,000 proteins deposited in the B
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: This study reveals that unassigned residues in NMR chemical shift datasets—often dismissed as noise—actually encode information about millisecond-scale protein dynamics. By curating over 100 NMR relaxation datasets and analyzing ~10,000 deposited protein datasets, the authors demonstrate that missing signals can serve as a large-scale, standardized observable for micro-to-millisecond conformational changes. This finding provides a new, broadly applicable resource for studying protein dynamics that was previously overlooked.

papers.title: MiRformer: a dual-transformer-encoder framework for predicting microRNA-mRNA interactions from paired sequences.
papers.abstract: MicroRNAs (miRNAs) regulate gene expression by binding to target messenger RNAs (mRNAs), inducing translational repression or mRNA degradation. Accurate prediction of miRNA-mRNA interactions and precise localization of binding and cleavage sites are critical for understanding post-transcriptional regulation and for enabling RNA therapeutics. Existing computational methods often rely on handcrafted or indirect features, scale poorly to kilobase-long mRNA sequences, or provide limited interpretability.
papers.summary: MiRformer is a new deep learning model that uses a dual-transformer-encoder architecture to predict which microRNAs bind to which messenger RNAs and precisely locate the binding sites, directly from the RNA sequences. It overcomes limitations of previous methods by avoiding handcrafted features and scaling efficiently to long mRNA sequences, while also providing interpretable results. This tool could improve our understanding of gene regulation and aid in designing RNA-based therapies.

papers.title: A novel transformer model of protein domains for viral taxonomy classification.
papers.abstract: Viruses with carefully curated taxonomic assignments (such as those in the ICTV taxonomy) still represent only a small fraction of viruses identified through sequencing data from virome or microbiome projects. It is therefore critical to develop methods that can assign viruses at multiple taxonomic ranks, so that a virus deemed novel at a given rank may still be placed into a higher-level taxon. Sequence-similarity-based approaches can classify viruses that share substantial genomic similarity with known viruses (e.g. those belonging to the same species or genus); however, their performance dr
papers.summary: This paper presents a novel transformer model that uses protein domain information to classify viruses into taxonomic ranks, even when they share little sequence similarity with known viruses. The approach addresses a key challenge in viromics: placing novel viruses into higher-level taxa when they cannot be assigned to species or genus. This could significantly improve automated viral classification from metagenomic data.

papers.title: NPBIP: predicting binding preferences of uncharacterized nucleic-acid-binding proteins.
papers.abstract: Nucleic-acid-binding proteins (NBPs) are crucial regulators of gene expression, recognizing specific RNA or DNA binding sites. While high-throughput experiments have generated vast amounts of binding data, a significant challenge remains in predicting binding affinities of a novel query NBP to any nucleic-acid sequence. Current computational methods often require prior experimental data for the query NBP or are limited to predictions over predefined short RNA or DNA sequences.
papers.summary: NPBIP is a new computational tool that can predict how any nucleic-acid-binding protein (NBP) will bind to RNA or DNA sequences, even if no prior experimental data exists for that protein. It overcomes limitations of existing methods that require pre-existing data or can only handle short sequences. This could help researchers quickly understand the binding preferences of uncharacterized NBPs.

papers.title: PU-GRAIL: residue-level graph learning for identifying protective bacterial antigens under positive-unlabeled supervision.
papers.abstract: The identification of protective antigens is fundamentally constrained by sparse annotations and pervasive label uncertainty in reverse vaccinology. Protective antigen-antibody interactions are mediated by a limited subset of surface-accessible residues that form spatially coherent epitopes. This motivates modeling antigenicity at the residue level, where 3D structure provides critical context for functional immune recognition. Moreover, antigen datasets are inherently positive unlabeled: unannotated proteins may contain hidden positives, making reliable negatives difficult to obtain.
papers.summary: PU-GRAIL is a new computational method that predicts protective bacterial antigens by analyzing protein structures at the residue level, using a graph neural network to identify surface regions that form epitopes. It handles the common problem of incomplete training data (positive-unlabeled learning) where many unannotated proteins may actually be protective antigens. The approach outperforms existing methods by leveraging 3D structural context rather than just sequence features.

papers.title: eGoT: enhanced graph-of-thoughts for multi-hop knowledge retrieval and hypothesis generation in biomedicine.
papers.abstract: Research on biological mechanisms and disease processes is limited by fragmented findings across unstructured text in publications. Question answering and hypothesis generation that can reason across multiple sources can overcome this limitation. However, Large language models (LLMs) are prone to inaccuracies and lack clear provenance to primary evidence. Retrieval augmented generation approaches that have provenance to the original source of evidence address these shortcomings. However, the response richness is dependent on the retrieval process design. Current approaches often fail to produc
papers.summary: This paper introduces eGoT, a graph-of-thoughts framework that enhances multi-hop knowledge retrieval and hypothesis generation in biomedicine by structuring evidence from fragmented literature. The approach improves upon standard retrieval augmented generation by providing clearer provenance to primary sources and enabling richer, more accurate reasoning across multiple documents. This addresses key limitations of large language models in biomedical question answering.

papers.title: Agentomics: an agentic system that autonomously develops novel state-of-the-art solutions for biomedical machine learning tasks.
papers.abstract: Extracting knowledge from biomedical data is crucial for advancing our understanding of biological systems and developing novel therapeutics. The quantity, quality, and resolution of biomedical data constantly evolves, requiring the automation of biomedical machine learning (ML). Existing Automated ML tools lack flexibility, while large language models (LLMs) struggle to consistently deliver reproducible machine learning codebases, and existing LLM Agent-powered solutions lag behind human-engineered ML models.
papers.summary: This paper introduces Agentomics, an AI system that autonomously designs and implements state-of-the-art machine learning solutions for biomedical problems, outperforming both existing automated ML tools and human-engineered models. The system leverages large language model agents to flexibly generate reproducible codebases tailored to evolving biomedical data.

papers.title: Predicting antibody self-association with sequence-structure fusion models: the central role of CSI-BLI in early developability screening.
papers.abstract: Antibody-based biologics are expanding rapidly, yet challenges in development from self-association, high viscosity, aggregation, and unfavorable clearance underscore the need for accurate in silico screening. Clone self-interaction biolayer interferometry (CSI-BLI) is a plate-based, low-material assay of weak, reversible self-association that serves as an early proxy for high-concentration viscosity and a complementary predictor of in vivo clearance. In a panel of 246 monoclonal antibodies, CSI-BLI moderately correlates with viscosity; further, in hFcRn Tg32 mice (41 antibodies), CSI-BLI stro
papers.summary: This study demonstrates that CSI-BLI, a plate-based assay measuring weak antibody self-association, serves as an early proxy for high-concentration viscosity and in vivo clearance. Using a panel of 246 monoclonal antibodies, the authors show that sequence-structure fusion models can predict CSI-BLI values, enabling early developability screening.

papers.title: Linguistics and human brain: a perspective of computational neuroscience.
papers.abstract: Elucidating the language-brain relationship requires bridging the methodological gap between linguistics' abstract theoretical frameworks and neuroscience's empirical neural data. As an interdisciplinary cornerstone, computational neuroscience formalizes language's hierarchical and dynamic structures into testable neural representation models through modeling, simulation, and data analysis, enabling computational dialogue between linguistic hypotheses and neural mechanisms. Recent advances in deep learning, particularly large language models (LLMs), have further advanced this inquiry: their hi
papers.summary: This perspective paper discusses how computational neuroscience can bridge the gap between linguistic theories and neural data by formalizing language structures into testable models. It highlights recent advances in deep learning, especially large language models (LLMs), as powerful tools for advancing our understanding of the language-brain relationship.

papers.title: Growth factor thermostability dataset derived from experimental and computational approaches to accelerate cultured meat development.
papers.abstract: This project compiled experimentally measured and computationally predicted thermostability data for growth factors (GFs) commonly used in cultured meat and seafood (CM) production. Data were generated on recombinant GFs using thermal shift assays and combined with publicly available data using a structured literature curation workflow developed using a Large Language Model (LLM) with manual validation. In addition, in silico protein stability calculations were generated using FoldX. Data such as GF identity, species origin, recombinant host, variant information, method of measurement, and exp
papers.summary: This paper presents a curated dataset of thermostability measurements for growth factors used in cultured meat production, combining experimental thermal shift assays, literature mining with LLM assistance, and computational predictions from FoldX. The resource aims to accelerate cultured meat development by providing stability data for key growth factors.

papers.title: Deep sequence learning with multi-task supervision for scalable population health monitoring.
papers.abstract: Models designed for artificial intelligence-driven population health monitoring must be able to integrate multiple types of information over time, be heterogeneous in the data they consider, and be deployed at scale. This research proposes an integrated multitask framework for predicting population disease incidence and mortality risk, based on deep learning and using both longitudinal biobank and National Health Survey data in a scalable manner. The framework will learn shared temporal representations from clinical lab-generated/demographically defined survey variables by applying a strict pr
papers.summary: This paper presents a deep learning framework that uses multi-task learning to predict population-level disease incidence and mortality risk from longitudinal biobank and survey data. The approach learns shared temporal representations across clinical and demographic variables, enabling scalable population health monitoring.

papers.title: Statistics and data science unlock the predictive power of quantitative genetics.
papers.abstract: Plant breeding is undergoing a profound transition from an empirical and phenotype-centered discipline toward a predictive, inferential, and decision-oriented science. This transformation has been driven not only by the generation of large-scale genomic, phenotypic, environmental, pangenomic, and multi-omics datasets, but also by the statistical and data science methodologies required to convert such information into actionable breeding decisions. Quantitative genetics remains the theoretical foundation for understanding genetic variance, heritability, genotype-by-environment (G×E) interaction
papers.summary: This review discusses how statistics and data science are transforming plant breeding from a phenotype-focused field into a predictive, data-driven science. It highlights the role of quantitative genetics in analyzing large-scale genomic, phenotypic, and multi-omics data to improve breeding decisions.

papers.title: Plasma proteomics based on machine learning predict the early risk of kidney outcomes in patients with DKD: a prospective cohort study from UK Biobank.
papers.abstract: Diabetic kidney disease (DKD) is the predominant cause of end-stage kidney disease worldwide. Early identification of patients at heightened risk for adverse kidney outcomes remains a critical unmet clinical need.
papers.summary: This study used machine learning on plasma proteomics data from the UK Biobank to build a predictive model for early kidney risk in patients with diabetic kidney disease. The model identified key proteins that can help clinicians identify high-risk patients earlier than current methods.

papers.title: Modality-level attribution and redundancy-aware radiomics for MRI-based differentiation of melanoma and NSCLC brain metastases.
papers.abstract: Differentiating the primary source of brain metastases using imaging alone is difficult, especially when intracranial disease is the initial sign of cancer. This study aimed to develop and validate a redundancy-aware, explainable radiomics framework for distinguishing melanoma from non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) brain metastases through multiparametric MRI.
papers.summary: This study presents a new radiomics framework that uses multiple MRI modalities to distinguish between melanoma and NSCLC brain metastases, with a focus on reducing redundant features and providing explanations for its predictions. The method could help clinicians identify the origin of brain metastases when the primary cancer is unknown.

papers.title: T-pGNN4DTI: Towards better drug-target interactions prediction using Global Self-attentive Pooled Graph Convolutional Networks and protein pre-training Models.
papers.abstract: Identification of drug-target interactions (DTI) is an important and challenging task in drug discovery and development. Traditional methods generally require biological experiments, which are costly and time-consuming. Machine learning-based methods can rapidly predict DTI using only computer algorithmic models, allowing researchers to validate only the most promising interactions through biochemical experiments. This holds promise for effectively addressing the current challenges of lengthy development cycles and high costs in new drug development. However, it is difficult for the existing D
papers.summary: This paper presents T-pGNN4DTI, a new machine learning method that combines graph neural networks with protein pre-training models to predict drug-target interactions more accurately. The approach uses global self-attentive pooling to better capture important features from drug and protein structures, potentially speeding up drug discovery by reducing the need for costly lab experiments.

papers.title: An interpretable AutoML-based prediction model for enteral nutrition intolerance in severe pulmonary tuberculosis patients and development of a clinical decision system.
papers.abstract: This study aims to construct an AutoML-based predictive model for enteral nutrition intolerance (ENI) in severe pulmonary tuberculosis (PTB) patients and develop a visualized clinical decision support system to inform personalized nutrition management.
papers.summary: This study uses automated machine learning (AutoML) to build a predictive model that identifies which severe pulmonary tuberculosis patients are likely to develop enteral nutrition intolerance, enabling personalized nutrition management. The authors also created a visual clinical decision support system to help clinicians apply the model in practice.

papers.title: Deep learning for cardiovascular disease: a comprehensive review of detection and risk forecasting.
papers.abstract: The biggest health threat to the global population is cardiovascular disease (CVD), which afflicts almost one-third of the global population and causes considerable monetary and social losses. Risk analysis should be performed in a timely and appropriate manner to enhance clinical practice and preventive interventions. The emergence of advanced data modalities, such as wearable sensors, medical imaging, electronic health records (EHRs), and genomic platforms, has led to a paradigm shift in the holistic assessment of CVD risk through multimodal data integration. This systematic review is a meth
papers.summary: This review summarizes how deep learning methods can integrate diverse data sources—including wearable sensors, medical imaging, electronic health records, and genomics—to improve the detection and risk prediction of cardiovascular disease. The authors highlight that such multimodal approaches offer a more holistic assessment of CVD risk, potentially enhancing clinical practice and preventive care.

papers.title: SLECA: A Single-Cell Atlas of Systemic Lupus Erythematosus Enabling Rare-Cell Discovery Using Graph Transformer.
papers.abstract: Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) is a complex autoimmune disease with multisystem involvement and marked interpatient heterogeneity. This heterogeneity has hindered precise characterization of the immune dysregulatory mechanisms underlying the disease. While rare immune cell populations are increasingly recognized as critical drivers of disease pathogenesis and progression, the lack of sufficiently powered, comprehensive single-cell transcriptomic resources has limited their systematic identification and characterization. To address this gap, we present SLECA, the first large-scale single-ce
papers.summary: SLECA is a large-scale single-cell atlas of systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) that uses a graph transformer model to identify rare immune cell populations, which are often missed in smaller studies. This resource helps uncover key immune dysregulation mechanisms in SLE by enabling systematic discovery of rare cells that may drive disease progression.

papers.title: PlantFormer: a precise plant disease segmentation network with interactive backbone and global-anisotropic context aggregation.
papers.abstract: Precise plant disease segmentation in real-world agricultural environments presents challenges that general-purpose models often fail to address, primarily due to the anisotropic spread of lesions, blurred biological boundaries, and severe background dominance. To overcome these bottlenecks, this paper proposes PlantFormer, an end-to-end network that effectively integrates and adapts advanced architectural components to address these domain-specific issues. Specifically, PlantFormer employs an InteractSwin Backbone with a Cross-Level Fusion (CLF) module to preserve early pathological details.
papers.summary: PlantFormer is a new deep learning network designed to accurately segment plant diseases in real-world agricultural images, overcoming challenges like irregular lesion shapes and blurred boundaries. It uses a specialized backbone and fusion module to preserve fine pathological details, improving segmentation performance over general-purpose models.

papers.title: GraphTransDTI: A novel hybrid framework combining graph transformer and CNN-BiLSTM for enhanced Drug-Protein Interaction prediction.
papers.abstract: Drug-protein interaction (DTI) prediction is a pivotal step in the drug discovery and repurposing process, helping to minimize experimental costs and time. However, existing deep learning methods often face limitations in simultaneously capturing the spatial structure of drug molecules and the deep contextual correlation with protein sequences. To address this issue, we propose GraphTransDTI, a synergistic hybrid framework that integrates a Graph Transformer to represent drug graph structures, a CNN-BiLSTM network to encode protein sequence context, and a Cross-Attention mechanism to model cro
papers.summary: GraphTransDTI is a new computational method that combines a Graph Transformer with CNN-BiLSTM and cross-attention to better predict how drugs interact with proteins. It improves upon existing deep learning approaches by simultaneously capturing the spatial structure of drug molecules and the contextual features of protein sequences.
